1.4:
We recommend that students begin with Ever Ancient, Ever New, Level 1. However, a brief review of material from Level 1 is covered in the early chapters of Level 2. This syllabus provides a breakdown in which the entire text of Level 2 is covered in a single year. There is a lot of material in Level 2 and students may wish to spread out the content here over two years. If so, the student can do Chapters 1-18 in the first year and Chapters 19-36 in the second year.

Review the Art History timeline on pp. viii and ix of Ever Ancient, Ever New, Level 2. (Note: your parents may wish to read the Note to Parents on pp. vi and viii. Then read Chapter 1 of Ever Ancient, Ever New, The Principles of Design. This first chapter outlines key terminology in the study of art you are beginning.

Please also take a moment to review the resources, attached to this syllabus, that are generic to the grade level and/or subject, e.g. the rubrics (these are found to the right of
1.5:
Optional: Do Art Pad #1, Color Wheel Spiral. These activities vary considerably in length, but they are an excellent opportunity to practice the art techniques that you are studying. Some activities may need to be spread out over several days. And some activities will build on the earlier activities. You may even wish to repeat some activities in order to improve your techniques.

Please note: if you are using this text for high school credit (either to supplement an existing Fine Arts course or by itself), it is important that you log your hours spent both reading the main text and doing art activities. You will need to submit this log of art hours at the end of each semester.

23.1:
Read the following selection and then paraphrase, that is, put in your own words, what it is saying:

"My little sisters, the birds, much bounden are ye unto God, your Creator, and always in every place ought ye to praise Him, for that He hath given you liberty to fly about everywhere, and hath also given you double and triple raiment; moreover He preserved your seed in the ark of Noah, that your race might not perish out of the world; still more are ye beholden to Him for the element of the air which He hath appointed for you; beyond all this, ye sow not, neither do you reap; and God feedeth you, and giveth you the streams and fountains for your drink; the mountains and the valleys for your refuge and the high trees whereon to make your nests; and because ye know not how to spin or sew, God clotheth you, you and your children; wherefore your Creator loveth you much, seeing that He Hath bestowed on you so many benefits; and therefore, my little sisters, beware of the sin of ingratitude, and study always to give praises unto God."

After paraphrasing the selection, answer this question in writing: Why doesn’t God clothe us as He does the birds?

Note: Please take a moment to review the resources, attached to this syllabus, that are generic to the grade level and/or subject, e.g. the rubrics (these are found to the right of

Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.2: Listen to Vivaldi’s “Four Seasons: Spring”. You could certainly listen to the whole work, all four sections, but it would be considerably longer.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.3: Have someone in your house play a minute section from the Canon in D and the Four Seasons; can you tell which one it is? Try this exercise several times.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.4: Listen to one of the Brandenburg Concertos.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.5: Have someone in your house play a minute section from the Four Seasons and the Brandenburg Concertos; can you tell which one it is? Try this exercise several times. Is it easier or harder to distinguish the Brandenburg Concerto from the Four Seasons than it was to distinguish the Concerto from the Canon in D?
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.6: Listen to the Tocatta and Fugue in D Minor.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.7: Have someone in your house play a minute section from the Tocatta and Fugue and from the Canon in D; can you tell which is which? Try this exercise several times. Is it easier or harder to distinguish between these two than it was to distinguish between the Brandenburg Concerto and the Four Seasons?
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.8: Now listen to the Water Music by Handel.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.9: Have someone play a short selection from the Water Music and a short selection from the Four Seasons; can you tell which is which? How about trying to distinguish between the Brandenburg Concerto and the Water Music? Can you do it? How about between the Water Music and the Canon in D? Which are the most alike and which the most different?
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.10: Listen to The Well Tempered Clavier by Bach.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.11: Listen to Piano Sonata no. 8 in C Minor by Beethoven.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.12: Have someone play a short selection from each of the last two pieces. Can you distinguish between them? Listen to a short selection from The Well Tempered Clavier and then from the Tocatta and Fugue in D minor. Can you distinguish between them? Which of these three pieces (Tocatta and Fugue, The Well Tempered Clavier, and the Piano Sonata no. 8) are most alike? Why?
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.13: Listen to the Oboe Concerto by Bach.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.14: Have someone play a short selection from each of the four Bach pieces we have listened to (Brandenburg Concerto, Tocatta and Fugue in D Minor, Oboe Concerto, and The Well Tempered Clavier). Let him choose the order, just calling each by a number. You try to put a name to each number. Can you do it?
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.15: Listen to the Christmas parts of the Messiah. If you have the libretto, it is easy to find the right parts. Otherwise, just listen to the words to tell where you are in Christ’s life. The words are in English, so you can tell when the music moves past the nativity. The Hallelujah chorus, with which nearly everyone is familiar, comes at the end of the work, after the passion and crucifixion, so we’ll save that for Easter.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.16: Listen to the String Quartet in C by Haydn.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.17: Listen to the Clarinet Quintet by Mozart.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.18: Have someone in your house play a three minute section from one of the last two pieces you have listened to; can you tell which one it is? Try this exercise several times.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.19: Have someone in your house play a short section from The Four Seasons and then from the Haydn string quartet; can you tell which one it is? Try this exercise several times.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.20: Listen to the Arrival of the Queen of Sheba by Handel.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.21: Have someone in your house play a short section from the Arrival of the Queen of Sheba and then from the Water Music; can you tell which is which? Then try to distinguish among the two just mentioned and the Haydn string quartet. Try this exercise several times.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.22: Listen to the Eine Kleine Nacht Music by Mozart.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.23: Listen to the Piano Sonata in C, K.545, by Mozart.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.24: Listen to the Clarinet Concerto in A Major by Mozart.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.25: Have someone play a short selection from each of the Mozart pieces we have listened to (Clarinet Quintet, Clarinet Concerto, Eine Kleine Nacht Music, Piano Sonata in C, K.545). Let him choose the order, just calling each by a number. You try to put a name to each number. Can you do it?
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.26: Listen to the Piano Sonata no. 8 in C Minor by Beethoven.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.27: Try to distinguish between Mozart’s Piano Sonata in C and Beethoven’s Piano Sonata no. 8 in C Minor. Can you do it? How would you characterize the difference?
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.29: Listen to Mozart’s Piano Concerto no. 23 in A Major.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.30: Now have someone play parts of the three piano pieces you have been listening to (Piano Sonata in C, K.545, Piano Concerto no. 23 in A Major, and Piano Sonata no. 8 in C Minor). Can you identify which is which? This is harder to do than in many of the previous exercises because the principle instrument is the same in all three pieces.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.31: This week have someone play the pieces from this year, in a random order, and try to identify as many of the pieces from the year as you can. Listen as long as you need to in order to identify the piece. Use the list from the front of this syllabus to help you.
Music 8 (Top 100 Classical Music 1685-19.32:
Try again to identify as many of the pieces from the year as you can. This time don’t use a list and see how you do.

Day 1: Read Chapter 1 of Our Life in the Church.

Note to parents: The most important thing you can do for your children is talk to them, and listen to them talk. A religion course is an excellent place to start a discussion that will touch on the very nature of reality. Take advantage of the opportunity. This syllabus is arranged so that discussions are suggested for days two and four. The student may write answers to the questions asked or suggested for discussion, but if it is possible to discuss, that would be better. There is a test given in
Day 2: Discuss the analogy of the orchestra on p. 13 (11) with your teacher. Be able to explain how the Catholic Church is like an orchestra. Look up Romans 12:6, 1 Corinthians 12, and Ephesians 4. What comparisons does St. Paul draw? Discuss Chapter 1 of Our Life in the Church with your teacher using the discussion questions in Chapter 1 Review in Our Life and the Church Quiz Book (see syllabus resources). The exercises could be used for additional discussion or to encourage independent work. (Note: one could also use the questions in Chapter 1, Lessons 1 & 2 of the activity book as a guide.)
Day 3: Read Chapter 2 in Our Life in the Church.
Day 4: Discuss Chapter 2 of Our Life in the Church with your teacher using the discussion questions in Chapter 2 Review in Our Life and the Church Quiz Book (see syllabus resources). The exercises could be used for additional discussion or to encourage independent work. (Note: one could also use the questions in Chapter 2, Lessons 1-3 of the activity book as a guide.)

The exercises could be used for additional discussion or to encourage independent work. (Note: one could also use the questions in Chapter 6, Lessons 1 & 3 of the activity book as a guide. And you could do the crossword puzzle in Lesson 4 if you have time).
Day 4: Work for 5 minutes on the memorization of #136-143, 153, 155, 147 & 148 in your Baltimore Catechism. Write a short essay answering the question, “How do you know Christ intended to establish a visible hierarchical Church?” A good answer will include the following points:

Jesus chose those who were to carry on His work, which means He intended it to be carried on. He even specified that this work was to embrace all nations.
The apostles understood this intention, for they chose another to take the place of the apostate Judas.
Further, in the Old Testament we see a visible hierarchical structure, and we know that the New Testament is a fulfillment of the Old Testament, so we would expect to see a similar hierarchical structure.
Additionally, the first council of the Church, the Council of Jerusalem, occurred in the lifetime of the apostles. They called this council as a means of resolving differences of opinion, and they all abided by the decisions of Peter and the council as a whole. They gave a weight to the conclusions of the council beyond an ordinary general agreement. In fact, who does St. Peter mention first in announcing the decisions of the Council? (Acts 15)

Based on St. Augustine’s explanation of this parable, explain the significance of the inn and the innkeeper. What other symbols (relating to this theme of grace) can you find in this parable?
Day 3: Work for 5 minutes on the memorization of #136-143, 153, 155, 147 & 148 in your Baltimore Catechism. Only the Catholic Church has the fullness of the truth.
Only the Catholic Church has the fullness of the means of salvation.
Only the Catholic Church has the authority from God which allows her to guide the faithful with certainty in matters of faith and morals.

How many points can you get? You can get one point of extra credit for each capital sin, cardinal virtue, spiritual work of mercy and corporal work of mercy you can name. 90-100 points is an A; 80-90 points is a B; 70-80 points is a C; 0-70 points means you better work harder!

You may take Religion Test II (see syllabus Resources, here), any time between now and the end of the year. Take it when you feel ready, and are confident of the new catechism questions.
Religion 8 (Our Life in the Church) Syll.27:
Day 1: Work for 5 minutes on reviewing catechism questions #304, 343, 347, 350, 351, 352, 379, 475, 476, 482, 483, 484, and 490. Read Part I, Chapter 1 of The Case for Christianity by C.S. Lewis (Note: The Case for Christianity is the first two parts of Mere Christianity).
Day 2: Work for 5 minutes on reviewing catechism questions #304, 343, 347, 350, 351, 352, 379, 475, 476, 482, 483, 484, and 490. Discuss the reading from yesterday with your teacher, using the following questions (also found in the eighth grade resource section of Designing Your Own Classical Curriculum):

C.S. Lewis makes two points in the first section. What are they? Which common way of behaving supports the first point, and which the second?
Day 3: Work for 5 minutes on reviewing catechism questions #304, 343, 347, 350, 351, 352, 379, 475, 476, 482, 483, 484, and 490. Read Part I, Chapter 2 of The Case for Christianity by C.S. Lewis.
Day 4: Work for 5 minutes on reviewing catechism questions #304, 343, 347, 350, 351, 352, 379, 475, 476, 482, 483, 484, and 490. Discuss the reading from yesterday with your teacher, using the following questions (also found in the eighth grade resource section of Designing Your Own Classical Curriculum):

In this section Lewis begins by giving three arguments to prove something about the Moral Law. What is he trying to establish?
Then he gives two reasons for thinking that the Law of Human Nature (or Moral Law) is a real truth (like math), not a convention (like traffic laws). What are the two reasons?
Do you understand the final point he makes about distinguishing between differences of morality and differences of belief about facts?
Religion 8 (Our Life in the Church) Syll.28:
Day 1: Work for 5 minutes on reviewing catechism questions #304, 343, 347, 350, 351, 352, 379, 475, 476, 482, 483, 484, and 490. Read Part I, Chapter 3 of The Case for Christianity by C.S. Lewis.
Day 2: Work for 5 minutes on reviewing catechism questions #304, 343, 347, 350, 351, 352, 379, 475, 476, 482, 483, 484, and 490. Discuss the reading from yesterday with your teacher, using the following questions (also found in the eighth grade resource section of Designing Your Own Classical Curriculum):

What makes the "Law of Human Nature" different from the laws of nature?
What examples show that the Moral Law (what a man ough to do) is different from what is convenient or profitable? Give two examples.
Summarize Lewis' summary, that is, list the points he has made about the Moral law.
Men ought to be _____.
The Moral Law is not a _____.
The Moral Law is not a _____.
The Moral Law is not a _____.
Therefore, _____.
Day 3: Work for 5 minutes on reviewing catechism questions #304, 343, 347, 350, 351, 352, 379, 475, 476, 482, 483, 484, and 490. Read Part I, Chapter 4 of The Case for Christianity by C.S. Lewis.
Day 4: Work for 5 minutes on reviewing catechism questions #304, 343, 347, 350, 351, 352, 379, 475, 476, 482, 483, 484, and 490. Discuss the reading from yesterday with your teacher, using the following questions (also found in the eighth grade resource section of Designing Your Own Classical Curriculum):

Copy the sentence in the first paragraph that best states what has been established by Lewis' reasoning so far.
What, roughly, are the two views of how the universe came to be? Can science answer the question, "Why is there a universe?"
If there is a power behind the things that are, that creates them and directs them, where would we look for evidence of it?
What three names does Lewis give for the in-between view he mentions in the note?
Religion 8 (Our Life in the Church) Syll.29:
Day 1: Work for 5 minutes on reviewing catechism questions #304, 343, 347, 350, 351, 352, 379, 475, 476, 482, 483, 484, and 490. Read Part I, Chapter 5 of The Case for Christianity by C.S. Lewis.
Day 2: Work for 5 minutes on reviewing catechism questions #304, 343, 347, 350, 351, 352, 379, 475, 476, 482, 483, 484, and 490. Discuss the reading from yesterday with your teacher, using the following questions (also found in the eighth grade resource section of Designing Your Own Classical Curriculum):

When do you make progress by going back?
What are the two "bits of evidence" we have, without the Bible, about Somebody behind the Moral Law?
Why is the second bit of evidence better?
How have we made ourselves the enemies of the Somebody who made the Moral Law?
Day 3: Work for 5 minutes on reviewing catechism questions #304, 343, 347, 350, 351, 352, 379, 475, 476, 482, 483, 484, and 490. Read Part II, Chapter 1 of The Case for Christianity by C.S. Lewis.
Day 4: Work for 5 minutes on reviewing catechism questions #304, 343, 347, 350, 351, 352, 379, 475, 476, 482, 483, 484, and 490. Discuss the reading from yesterday with your teacher, using the following questions (also found in the eighth grade resource section of Designing Your Own Classical Curriculum):

How is a Christian more tolerant of other religions than an athiest?
Why is it that an athiest, who does not believe that the universe or anything in it was designed by Someone on purpose, cannot trust his own thinking to be true?
The first division of humanity is into those who believe in God and those who do not. Of those who believe in God, the next division is according to the sort of God they beleive in. What two sorts are there?
Why is Christianity a fighting religion?
How does the injustice of the world prove that there must be a God?
Religion 8 (Our Life in the Church) Syll.30:
Day 1: Work for 5 minutes on reviewing catechism questions #304, 343, 347, 350, 351, 352, 379, 475, 476, 482, 483, 484, and 490. Read Part II, Chapter 2 of The Case for Christianity by C.S. Lewis.
Day 2: Work for 5 minutes on reviewing catechism questions #304, 343, 347, 350, 351, 352, 379, 475, 476, 482, 483, 484, and 490. Discuss the reading from yesterday with your teacher, using the following questions (also found in the eighth grade resource section of Designing Your Own Classical Curriculum):

What is the Christian explanation of "a universe that contains much that is obviously bad and meaningless, but containing creatures who know it is bad and meaningless"?
If we distinguish between a Good Power and a Bad Power in the universe, what third thing do we need in order to judge which is which?
Since Christianity is not Dualism, how does it explain the existence of a dark power?
Day 3: Work for 5 minutes on reviewing catechism questions #304, 343, 347, 350, 351, 352, 379, 475, 476, 482, 483, 484, and 490. Read Part II, Chapter 3 of The Case for Christianity by C.S. Lewis.
Day 4: Work for 5 minutes on reviewing catechism questions #304, 343, 347, 350, 351, 352, 379, 475, 476, 482, 483, 484, and 490. Discuss the reading from yesterday with your teacher, using the following questions (also found in the eighth grade resource section of Designing Your Own Classical Curriculum):

What did God give us that made it possible for the world to have evil in it?
What was the sin of Satan and the sin he taught to us?
What three things (before Jesus' coming) did God do to counteract the evil Satan did?
What three claims of Christ were "talking as if he was God"?
Religion 8 (Our Life in the Church) Syll.31:
Day 1: Work for 5 minutes on reviewing catechism questions #304, 343, 347, 350, 351, 352, 379, 475, 476, 482, 483, 484, and 490. Read Part II, Chapter 4 of The Case for Christianity by C.S. Lewis.
Day 2: Work for 5 minutes on reviewing catechism questions #304, 343, 347, 350, 351, 352, 379, 475, 476, 482, 483, 484, and 490. Discuss the reading from yesterday with your teacher, using the following questions (also found in the eighth grade resource section of Designing Your Own Classical Curriculum):

What did Jesus come mainly to do?
If it is necessary to have a complete understanding of Christ's sacrifice, what is necessary to be believed?
What is the "catch" in repentance?
Day 3: Work for 5 minutes on reviewing catechism questions #304, 343, 347, 350, 351, 352, 379, 475, 476, 482, 483, 484, and 490. Read Part II, Chapter 5 of The Case for Christianity by C.S. Lewis.
Day 4: Work for 5 minutes on reviewing catechism questions #304, 343, 347, 350, 351, 352, 379, 475, 476, 482, 483, 484, and 490. Discuss the reading from yesterday with your teacher, using the following questions (also found in the eighth grade resource section of Designing Your Own Classical Curriculum):

What three things spread Christ's life to us?
What does "believing things on authority" mean?
If we receive the Christ-life by these three things, why are we obliged to do anything else?
The Christian doesn't think God will love us because we're good, but that...

Day 4: Nature journaling is important. A good scientist can notice details; he pays attention to what he is studying. When you begin to journal, decide first what your objective is:

 Are you observing plants? Animals? Birds? Flowers? Insects? Landscapes? Decide that first.

You will need: a nature journal (a blank sketchbook), a drawing pencil, a sharper, color pencils if you want to focus on details with color, binoculars if you are watching birds. You may choose to use a camera to document what you see. A field guide book for your area may be helpful. If you are studying plants, a crayon to do “leaf rubs” is a good addition.

 Always note the location, time and day in your journal for each entry. Note your observations next to sketches. If you take pictures, you can paste them in the journal next to your notes.

 Spend the next three days doing journaling. If you like it, you can continue it throughout the year.

Spelling & Vocabulary 8 (Vocabulary from.1:
Day 1: Look over the Table of Contents, Preface and Notes. Then look at P. , Motion (Lesson 1). First look at the meanings of the roots ‘per’, ‘fero’, ‘tendo’. Then look at the Lesson 1 Key Words. Which do you know without looking at the definitions? Use the roots to help you. Read over the entries for each of the key words. Start a notebook in which you put the words, so that you practice spelling them. Write down definitions for any words you do not already know.
Day 2: Look at the Familiar Words and Challenge Words for each root word in Lesson 1. If there are any you do not know, or can’t figure out, look them up. You can add them to your notebook. Go over any of the Key Words you had trouble spelling.
Day 3: Do the Lesson 1 Exercises. Go over any Lesson 1 Key Words you had trouble spelling.
Day 4: Have someone read you the definition of the Lesson 1 Key Words and you write down the word. If you had trouble spelling any of them, practice.
Day 5: Take a spelling test on Lesson 1 Key words. If there are definitions you are unsure of, read them over again.

1.1:
Read “Macbeth” in Charles and Mary Lamb’s Tales from Shakespeare. Divide the number of pages this story takes by four, and read that many pages each day this week.

Review the poems you learned in Kindergarten (see the Poetry List for Kindergarten to Eighth Grade found at the end of these lesson plans). Recite as many as you can from memory. If you have not already memorized these poems or cannot recite them, that is fine, just read and enjoy them.

Oral Drill: conjugate “regere” and “audire” in the present, imperfect, and future tenses. Do the Day 1 exercises. Practice vocabulary for ten minutes.

rego, regis, regit, regimus, regitis, regunt
regebam, regebas, regebat, regebamus, regebatis, regebant
regam, reges, reget, regemus, regetis, regent
regam, regas, regat, regamus, regatis, regant
audio, audis, audit, audimus, auditis, audunt

audiam, audies, audiet, audiemus, audietis, audient
audiam, audias, audiat, audiamus, audiatis, audiant

Lesson 8. Review adverbs (lesson VII). Read the eighth lesson. Oral Drill: decline the adjective “fortis/e,” then decline the noun, “dux, ducis,” and then put the two together, remembering that “dux” is a masculine word. Do the Day 1 exercises. Practice vocabulary for ten minutes.

Declining an adjective and a noun together orally is a very difficult task, because the student has to be able to remember two different declensions at the same time. If the student has trouble, he can write out the two words and then say them out loud. Or, he could write out one of the words to help him along, and then decline out loud. Or, he could just make a note to himself where the adjective and noun endings will be different and then decline out loud. In all cases, its important for him to hear the noun-adjective combination as well as see it on paper.

Oral Drill Answer Key:

m/f: fortis, fortis, forti, fortem, forti, fortes, fortium, fortibus, fortes, fortibus
n: forte, fortis, forti, forte, forti, fortia, fortium, fortibus, fortia, fortibus
dux, ducis, duci, ducem, duce, duces, ducum, ducibus, duces, ducibus
fortis dux, fortis ducis, forti duci, fortem ducem, forti duce, fortes duces, fortium ducum, fortibusducibus, fortes duces, fortibus ducibus

Review Lesson A. Do Oral Review. Practice vocabulary for ten minutes.

Oral Review:

Say what gender these words have: gens, gentis; dux, ducis; mens, mentis; flumen, fluminis; mons, montis; mare, maris. (gens, mens — feminine; dux, mons — masculine; flumen, mare — neuter)
Now say which of these words are i-stem: gens, gentis; dux, ducis; mens, mentis; flumen, fluminis; mons, montis; mare, maris. (i-stem: gens, mens, mons, mare)
Decline the reflexive pronoun “--, sui.” (--, sui, sibi, se, se)
Which parts of speech are indeclinable? (preposition, interjection, conjunction, adverb)
How do you form the present tense of “ere” verbs? (find the stem, change the “e” to “i,” add the endings. The first person singular and third person plural are irregulars.)
How do the form the imperfect tense of “ere” verbs? (find the stem, add “ba,” add the endings.)
How do you form the future tense of “ere” verbs? (find the stem, add the endings. The first person singular is irregular.)
How do you form the future possible tense of “ere” verbs? (find the stem, change the “e” to an “a,” add the endings.)
Why are “ire” verbs called copycat i-lovers? (because they copycat the “ere” verbs but keep their i’s)

Oral Review:

Say which gender these nouns have: clamor, clamoris; caput, capitis; collis, collis; Caesar, Caesaris; pons, pontis. (clamor, collis, Caesar — masculine; pons — masculine; caput — neuter)
Say which of these nouns are i-stem: clamor, clamoris; caput, capitis; collis, collis; Caesar, Caesaris; pons, pontis. (collis, pons)
In a Latin sentence, where does the verb usually go? (at the end)
Decline the reflexive pronoun “--, sui.” (--, sui, sibi, se, se)
How do you find the stem of a first declension noun? (take the “ae” off the second form)
What is the genitive singular of these nouns: terrae, terrae; pax, pacis; caput, capitis? accusative singular? dative plural? (terrae, pacis, capitis; terram, pacem, caput; terris, pacibus, capitibus)
What three things must adjectives modify their nouns in? Does this mean that the endings of adjectives will match the endings of nouns? (gender, case, and number. No, the endings do not necessarily match, because the endings for adjectives are not always the same as for nouns.)

1.1: ). Some of the PDF files found here are taken from the printed copy of the corresponding syllabus, if there is one. There are also resources specific to the particular course that will be assigned or referenced within the syllabus assignments. Answer Keys or Assignment overviews for the syllabus, if applicable, are only found via the parent log-in; the student log-in may access all other syllabus resources.
1.4:
We recommend that students begin with Ever Ancient, Ever New, Level 1. However, a brief review of material from Level 1 is covered in the early chapters of Level 2. This syllabus provides a breakdown in which the entire text of Level 2 is covered in a single year. There is a lot of material in Level 2 and students may wish to spread out the content here over two years. If so, the student can do Chapters 1-18 in the first year and Chapters 19-36 in the second year.

Review the Art History timeline on pp. viii and ix of Ever Ancient, Ever New, Level 2. (Note: your parents may wish to read the Note to Parents on pp. vi and viii. Then read Chapter 1 of Ever Ancient, Ever New, The Principles of Design. This first chapter outlines key terminology in the study of art you are beginning.

Please also take a moment to review the resources, attached to this syllabus, that are generic to the grade level and/or subject, e.g. the rubrics (these are found to the right of
1.5:
Optional: Do Art Pad #1, Color Wheel Spiral. These activities vary considerably in length, but they are an excellent opportunity to practice the art techniques that you are studying. Some activities may need to be spread out over several days. And some activities will build on the earlier activities. You may even wish to repeat some activities in order to improve your techniques.

Please note: if you are using this text for high school credit (either to supplement an existing Fine Arts course or by itself), it is important that you log your hours spent both reading the main text and doing art activities. You will need to submit this log of art hours at the end of each semester.

2.4: Read Unit 1, Chapter 2, Naturalism in the Italian Renaissance.

1.1:
Read “Macbeth” in Charles and Mary Lamb’s Tales from Shakespeare. Divide the number of pages this story takes by four, and read that many pages each day this week.

2.1:
Start memorizing “Macbeth”, Act V, Scene V, Lines 19-27 (this passage begins with "She should have died hereafter..."). Spend five minutes each day this week working on the first two lines.

Review the poems you learned in Kindergarten (see the Poetry List for Kindergarten to Eighth Grade found at the end of these lesson plans). Recite as many as you can from memory. If you have not already memorized these poems or cannot recite them, that is fine, just read and enjoy them.

3.2: Memorize and review for five minutes.
3.4: Memorize and review for five minutes, then recite as much of the section as you now know.

Work on the next two lines, up to “... brief candle”, and review the previous lines.

Review the poems you learned in First Grade (see the Poetry List for Kindergarten to Eighth Grade found at the end of these lesson plans). Recite as many as you can from memory. If you have not already memorized these poems or cannot recite them, that is fine, just read and enjoy them.
4.2: Memorize and review for five minutes.
4.4: Memorize and review for five minutes, then recite as much of the section as you now know.

Spelling & Vocabulary 8 (Vocabulary from.1:
Day 1: Look over the Table of Contents, Preface and Notes. Then look at P. , Motion (Lesson 1). First look at the meanings of the roots ‘per’, ‘fero’, ‘tendo’. Then look at the Lesson 1 Key Words. Which do you know without looking at the definitions? Use the roots to help you. Read over the entries for each of the key words. Start a notebook in which you put the words, so that you practice spelling them. Write down definitions for any words you do not already know.
Day 2: Look at the Familiar Words and Challenge Words for each root word in Lesson 1. If there are any you do not know, or can’t figure out, look them up. You can add them to your notebook. Go over any of the Key Words you had trouble spelling.
Day 3: Do the Lesson 1 Exercises. Go over any Lesson 1 Key Words you had trouble spelling.
Day 4: Have someone read you the definition of the Lesson 1 Key Words and you write down the word. If you had trouble spelling any of them, practice.
Day 5: Take a spelling test on Lesson 1 Key words. If there are definitions you are unsure of, read them over again.
Spelling & Vocabulary 8 (Vocabulary from.2:
Day 1: Begin Lesson 2. First look at the meanings of the roots for Lesson 2. Then look at the Lesson 2 Key Words. Which do you know without looking at the definitions? Use the roots to help you. Read over the entries for each of the key words. Add the key words to your notebook, so that you practice spelling them. Write down definitions for any words you do not already know.
Day 2: Look at the Familiar Words and Challenge Words for each root word in Lesson 2. If there are any you do not know, or can’t figure out, look them up. You can add them to your notebook. Go over any of the Key Words you had trouble spelling.
Day 3: Do the Lesson 2 Exercises. Go over any Lesson 2 Key Words you had trouble spelling.
Day 4: Have someone read you the definition of the Lesson 2 Key Words and you write down the word. If you had trouble spelling any of them, practice.
Day 5: Take a spelling test on the Lesson 2 Key words. If there are definitions you are unsure of, read them over again.

History & Geog 7 (Old World & America) S.1:
Day 1: ). Some of the PDF files found here are taken from the printed copy of the corresponding syllabus, if there is one. There are also resources specific to the particular course that will be assigned or referenced within the syllabus assignments. Answer Keys or Assignment overviews for the syllabus, if applicable, are only found via the parent log-in; the student log-in may access all other syllabus resources.
Day 1: If you do not have a timeline already, now is the time to construct one. (Note that there are many relevant dates for your timeline listed in Designing Your Own Classical Curriculum.) Please see the resources attached to this syllabus, especially the Timeline for History Example and Timeline for History for more detailed instructions. 

Note: Please take a moment to review the resources, attached to this syllabus, that are generic to the grade level and/or subject, e.g. the rubrics (these are found to the right of
Day 2: Finish Timeline.
Day 3: Locate a good map of Mediterranean Area. The maps on p. 6 and pp. 10-11 of Historical Atlas of the World by Rand McNally are good maps for this exercise. Locate Assyria, Egypt, Greece, & Phoenicia. Think about where these countries are in relation to each other and how someone would have to travel to go from one to the next. Then locate a good map of Greece. The "Outline Map of Greece" and the "Color Map of Greece" in your syllabus resources were developed for this project. Make sure your map includes the nearby islands and the western edge of modern Turkey. (Note: The map on p. 7 of Historical Atlas of the World by Rand McNally, The Greeks, p. 81, Old World and America p. 31 also have good maps of Ancient Greece. Trace (or draw freehand) the outline of Greece and the surrounding area. Photocopy it 7 times.
Day 4: Read pp. 25-35 in Old World and America. Do the Objective Tests, and when you give the answer, also give the page number on which the answer is found.
History & Geog 7 (Old World & America) S.2:
Day 1: Read pp. 10-21 (The Mycenaeans, Mycenaean Cities, The Trojan War, The End of the Age, The Dark Ages, The Archaic Period, The Greeks and Their Neighbors, The Social Structure and Government) in The Greeks. Enter relevant information on your timeline.
Day 2: Read ‘Draco and Solon’ in Famous Men of Greece, pp. 75-78 (pp. 59-62).
Day 3: Locate on your original outline map of Greece: Aegean Sea, Mediterranean Sea, Ionian Sea, Mt. Olympus, Athens, Crete, Hellespont, Thebes, Ithaca, Sparta, Troy (Ilium), Thessaly, Mycenae, Pylos, Delphi, Thermopylae, Marathon, Plataea, Salamis, Corinth, Olympia.
Day 4: Read ‘Pisistratus’ in Famous Men of Greece, pp. 79-80 (pp. 63-64).
History & Geog 7 (Old World & America) S.3:
Day 1: Read pp. 22-23 (Sparta), & pp. 60-63 (Democracy in Athens, The Golden Age and the Peloponnesian War) in The Greeks. Enter relevant information on your timeline.
Day 2: Read the chapters on Lycurgus and Pericles in Lives from Plutarch: The Modern American Edition of Twelve. If you don’t have this, then read ‘Lycurgus’ in Famous Men of Greece, pp. 69-71 (pp. 55-58), and ‘Pericles’ in Famous Men of Greece, pp. 111-118 (pp. 85-88). (If you have Theras and His Town by Caroline Snedeker (American Home School Publishing), this is a good place to read it. I highly recommend this book.)
Day 3: Do Questions That Make You Think, p. 36, in The Old World and America. Do five of the ten questions. Give your answers in complete sentences, and give reasons for your answers. Ideally each answer should be a short paragraph with a topic sentence and several supporting sentences. A good answer should always be at least three sentences long. Also, give the page on which you found the answer.
Day 4: Do Strange Facts, pp. 36-37, in The Old World and America. Remember, each answer should be three or more sentences long. Give the page number on which you found the answer.

(Note: the text says "How many of the following can you explain." Students are being asked to answer/explain all six of these statements. The answers can be found in the chapter.)
History & Geog 7 (Old World & America) S.4:
Day 1: Take one of the photocopies of your original map and, without looking, try to locate the following: Aegean Sea, Mediterranean Sea, Ionian Sea, Mt. Olympus, Athens, Crete, Hellespont, Thebes, Ithaca, Sparta, Troy (Ilium), Thessaly, Mycenae, Pylos, Delphi, Thermopylae, Marathon, Plataea, Salamis, Corinth, Olympia. After you have done all you can from memory, enter the rest after looking at your previous filled in map.
Day 2: Read pp. 38-48 in The Old World and America. Then read pp. 36-41 (The Army, The Navy, The Persian Wars) in The Greeks, and ‘Miltiades, the Hero of Marathon’ in Famous Men of Greece, pp. 81-83 (pp. 65-67). (Note: If you have Temple on a Hill by Anne Rockwell (unfortunately, out of print), or The Lion in the Gateway by Mary Renault, now is the time to read it.)
Day 3: Read Book Six, lines 94-120, in The Histories. (The battle of Marathon). This is found on pp. 424-434 in the 2015 Penguin Classics edition, [pp. 394-403 in the 2003, pp. 355-364 in 1996]. Many seventh graders find this a difficult text to read. I have found that reading it to the children as a family read aloud can help.
Day 4: Read ‘Leonidas at Thermopylae’ in Famous Men of Greece, pp. 87-89 (pp. 69-71).
History & Geog 7 (Old World & America) S.5:
Day 1: Read Book Seven, lines 175-239, in The Histories. (Thermopylae) This is found on pp. 511-534 in the 2015 Penguin Classics edition, [pp. 477-499 in the 2003, pp. 429-450 in 1996].
Day 2: Read the chapters on Aristides and Cimon in Lives from Plutarch: The Modern American Edition of Twelve. Read ‘Themistocles’, ‘Aristides the Just’ and ‘Cimon’ in Famous Men of Greece, pp. 95-110 (pp. 73-84). Note: You only need to read Aristides and Cimon in one of the two texts.
Day 3: Read Book Eight, lines 1-32 and 34-39, in The Histories. (Salamis) This is found on pp. 535-547 in the 2015 Penguin Classics edition, [pp. 501-513 in the 2003, pp. 451-462 in 1996]. Note to Parents: Line 33 may be disturbing to some students and you may wish to black this out.
Day 4: Read Book Eight, lines 40-100, in The Histories. (Salamis) This is found on pp. 547-568 in the 2015 Penguin Classics edition, [pp. 513-535 in the 2003, pp. 462-481 in 1996].

Day 2: Read Book Nine, lines 1-75 in The Histories. (Plataea) This is found on pp. 589-620 in the 2015 Penguin Classics edition, [pp. 554-585 in the 2003, pp. 499-526 in 1996].

Augustus Caesar's World has a passage that casts doubt on the historicity of the Gospels. We encourage parents to read this Paragraph 19 of Verbum Dei with their students to clarify the truth of this point:

"Holy Mother Church has firmly and with absolute constancy held, and continues to hold, that the four Gospels just named, whose historical character the Church unhesitatingly asserts, faithfully hand on what Jesus Christ, while living among men, really did and taught for their eternal salvation until the day He was taken up into heaven (see Acts 1:1). Indeed, after the Ascension of the Lord the Apostles handed on to their hearers what He had said and done. This they did with that clearer understanding which they enjoyed (3) after they had been instructed by the glorious events of Christ's life and taught by the light of the Spirit of truth. (2) The sacred authors wrote the four Gospels, selecting some things from the many which had been handed on by word of mouth or in writing, reducing some of them to a synthesis, explaining some things in view of the situation of their churches and preserving the form of proclamation but always in such fashion that they told us the honest truth about Jesus.(4) For their intention in writing was that either from their own memory and recollections, or from the witness of those who "themselves from the beginning were eyewitnesses and ministers of the Word" we might know "the truth" concerning those matters about which we have been instructed (see Luke 1:2-4)."

Music 7 (Alfred's Essentials of Music) S.1:
Day 1: ). Some of the PDF files found here are taken from the printed copy of the corresponding syllabus, if there is one. There are also resources specific to the particular course that will be assigned or referenced within the syllabus assignments. Answer Keys or Assignment overviews for the syllabus, if applicable, are only found via the parent log-in; the student log-in may access all other syllabus resources.
Day 3: The emphasis this year is on becoming well acquainted with a small number of great and easily accessible works of sacred music. A secondary goal will be to further the student’s understanding of music theory and basic music skills. Seventh Grade music builds upon the previous years. If you have not done Fifth or Sixth Grade Music you may want to add in Lessons 1-12 of music theory to learn previous concepts (though some review is always provided each year). Students entering in 7th Grade without previous musical background, may want to consider doing the Sixth Grade music. (If you would like to hear an excellent talk on the virtues of classical music, get Andrew Pudewa’s talk “Profound Effect of Music on Life”. This can be found here: https://iew.com/the-profound-effects-of-music-on-life-andrew-pudewa.)

The classical pieces assigned in the syllabus are favorites of my family. They can be found in a variety of places, such as YouTube, Amazon, Apple Music, Pandora, Classical Archives, and so forth. You may be able to subscribe for unlimited music or purchase selected songs. Please be aware that free sites or sites with radio stations (like Pandora) will occasionally display advertisements of questionable moral content; parents are encouraged to supervise their children’s use, as with any website.

Religion 7 (Acts of the Apostles) Syllab.5:
Day 1: You will be reading and discussing Chapters 1 and 2 of Acts this week. The questions for discussion are in the syllabus resources (here). Today read Chapter 1. Also, start working on two new Friendly Defender cards. Pick one card from each of the following categories: Confession and Church. Read the cards and think about the issues. Start to memorize the verses.
Day 2: Discuss the questions for Chapter 1. Or, if you prefer, you may write out your answers.

What is the full title of Acts?
The book begins right after an important event, or series of events. What is it? or What are they?
Jesus gave the apostles a final instruction. What did He say? And what happened next?
To what city do the apostles go after the Ascension?
How was Matthias chosen to be an apostle? Whose place did he take?
Why does Peter conduct the election?
Review your new cards, both the questions and answers and the Bible verses. Also review one card from last week.
Day 3: Read Chapter 2 of Acts. Also, review your cards, both the questions and answers and the Bible verses. Review one card from last week.
Day 4: Discuss the questions for Chapter 2, or write out your answers.

Which came first, Pentecost or the Ascension?
Describe the events of the coming of the Holy Spirit. They were together in one place and…(1, 2, 3, 4)
People heard about this amazing event and gathered together. What surprised them?
Who first said, “And it shall come to pass in the last days (saith the Lord), I will pour out my Spirit upon all flesh…”? What was he talking about?
Who first said, “Thou [God] wilt not leave my soul in hell, nor suffer they Holy One to see corruption…”?
To whom did he refer?
Why did Peter tell the people that Joel and David were talking about Christ?
What did Peter tell the Jews that they should do?
How many were converted that day?
What signs of charity and divine favor attended the first Christian community?
Practice defending your faith. Have someone ask you one or more of the questions you have been working on in your Friendly Defender cards. Answer the question and give the Bible verse that pertains to that answer.
Religion 7 (Acts of the Apostles) Syllab.6:
Day 1: You will be reading and discussing Chapters 3 and 4 of Acts this week. The questions for discussion are in Designing Your Own Classical Curriculum. Today read Chapter 3. Also, start working on two new Friendly Defender cards. Pick one card from each of the following categories: Tradition and Salvation. Read the cards and think about the issues. Start to memorize the verses.
Day 2: Discuss the questions for Chapter 3. Or, if you prefer, you may write out your answers.

Speaking in tongues preceded Peter’s great sermon in chapter 2. Here, another miracle precedes Peter’s second great sermon. What is it?
How does Peter heal a man? What does Peter say about how the man was healed?
In this sermon what does Peter exhort the people to do?
Go over your new Friendly Defender cards. Learn the questions and answers; work on memorizing the Bible verses. Also review two cards from last week.
Day 3: Read Chapter 4 of Acts. Review your new cards, both the questions and answers and the Bible verses. Also review two cards from last week.
Day 4: Discuss the questions for Chapter 4, or write out your answers.

How many people had now come to believe in our Lord?
Who apprehends Peter and John? What does Peter say that makes them angry?
What did the council tell the apostles to do? Did Peter and John agree to this?
When Peter and John went back to their own company, what happened?
Practice defending your faith. Have someone ask you one or more of the questions you have been working on in your Friendly Defender cards. Answer the question and give the Bible verse that pertains to that answer.

What was the sin of Ananias and Sapphira?
Why were the apostles apprehended the second time?
Did they stay in prison?
Why did Peter disobey the order of the council not to preach the Name of Jesus?
Who urged moderation on the council, and what was he?
What punishment did the apostles receive?
Review your new cards, both the questions and answers and the Bible verses. Also review three cards from last week.
Day 3: Read Chapter 6 of Acts. Review your new cards, both the questions and answers and the Bible verses. Also review three cards from last week.
Day 4: Discuss the questions for Chapter 6, or write out your answers.

For what service were Stephen and six others selected?
With what ceremony were they selected?
In what ways was Stephen outstanding in the Christian community?
Of what did false witnesses accuse him?
What indicates the Stephen’s speech in response was inspired by the Holy Spirit?
Practice defending your faith. Have someone ask you one or more of the questions you have been working on in your Friendly Defender cards. Answer the question and give the Bible verse that pertains to that answer.

And what did the Jewish leaders of his day do?
What was Stephen’s last act?
Why do you think the Roman government was not involved in this first persecution of the Church?

Day 3: Read Chapter 8 of Acts. Review your new cards, both the questions and answers and the Bible verses. Also review three cards you worked on previously.

Who preached the good news of Christ to the Samaritans?
How did they receive it?
What did Peter and John add to the baptisms in Samaria?
What was Simon’s sin?
Why did Philip leave Samaria?
What was God’s plan for him on the road to Gaza?

What did Saul do after he regained his sight?
Where did Saul begin to preach?
Why did he leave? How?
Why were the disciples afraid of Saul in Jerusalem?
What miracle did Peter work at Lydda?
Who was Tabitha?
What did Peter do for her?

What did the voice tell Peter after his vision of the animals?

What did those who came with Peter see that showed them that the Holy Spirit had indeed come upon the gentiles there?
What does “those of circumcision” mean?

The 101 Super Simple Science Experiments is a great book because the experiments are easy to do and the materials can be found around the house. Each experiment is written in a lab report format, which helps the students to learn how to write good lab reports. Encourage the student to make his own lab report for each experiment, writing down the materials he will use, what his goal is in the experiment, and what he observes. rego, regis, regit, regimus, regitis, regunt

audiam, audias, audiat, audiamus, audiatis, audiant

Lesson 8. Review adverbs (lesson VII). Read the eighth lesson. Oral Drill: decline the adjective “fortis/e,” then decline the noun, “dux, ducis,” and then put the two together, remembering that “dux” is a masculine word. Do the Day 1 exercises. Practice vocabulary for ten minutes.

Declining an adjective and a noun together orally is a very difficult task, because the student has to be able to remember two different declensions at the same time. If the student has trouble, he can write out the two words and then say them out loud. Or, he could write out one of the words to help him along, and then decline out loud. Or, he could just make a note to himself where the adjective and noun endings will be different and then decline out loud. In all cases, its important for him to hear the noun-adjective combination as well as see it on paper.

Oral Drill Answer Key:

m/f: fortis, fortis, forti, fortem, forti, fortes, fortium, fortibus, fortes, fortibus
n: forte, fortis, forti, forte, forti, fortia, fortium, fortibus, fortia, fortibus
dux, ducis, duci, ducem, duce, duces, ducum, ducibus, duces, ducibus
fortis dux, fortis ducis, forti duci, fortem ducem, forti duce, fortes duces, fortium ducum, fortibusducibus, fortes duces, fortibus ducibus

9.1: Attend Class. Do the Lesson VIII Quia Games.
9.2:
Review Lesson A. Do Oral Review. Practice vocabulary for ten minutes.

Oral Review:

Say what gender these words have: gens, gentis; dux, ducis; mens, mentis; flumen, fluminis; mons, montis; mare, maris. (gens, mens — feminine; dux, mons — masculine; flumen, mare — neuter)
Now say which of these words are i-stem: gens, gentis; dux, ducis; mens, mentis; flumen, fluminis; mons, montis; mare, maris. (i-stem: gens, mens, mons, mare)
Decline the reflexive pronoun “--, sui.” (--, sui, sibi, se, se)
Which parts of speech are indeclinable? (preposition, interjection, conjunction, adverb)
How do you form the present tense of “ere” verbs? (find the stem, change the “e” to “i,” add the endings. The first person singular and third person plural are irregulars.)
How do the form the imperfect tense of “ere” verbs? (find the stem, add “ba,” add the endings.)
How do you form the future tense of “ere” verbs? (find the stem, add the endings. The first person singular is irregular.)
How do you form the future possible tense of “ere” verbs? (find the stem, change the “e” to an “a,” add the endings.)
Why are “ire” verbs called copycat i-lovers? (because they copycat the “ere” verbs but keep their i’s)

The student text for this lesson says: We will spend some time working with what we know so far, building fluency with Latin translation and forms. Over the next several weeks we will do many translations, some from English to Latin, following our standard format, and then adapting to a new format. Some Latin to English translations will be added. There will be lots of new vocabulary introduced as well and these words will be used in simple and complex translations.

Oral Review:

Say which gender these nouns have: clamor, clamoris; caput, capitis; collis, collis; Caesar, Caesaris; pons, pontis. (clamor, collis, Caesar — masculine; pons — masculine; caput — neuter)
Say which of these nouns are i-stem: clamor, clamoris; caput, capitis; collis, collis; Caesar, Caesaris; pons, pontis. (collis, pons)
In a Latin sentence, where does the verb usually go? (at the end)
Decline the reflexive pronoun “--, sui.” (--, sui, sibi, se, se)
How do you find the stem of a first declension noun? (take the “ae” off the second form)
What is the genitive singular of these nouns: terrae, terrae; pax, pacis; caput, capitis? accusative singular? dative plural? (terrae, pacis, capitis; terram, pacem, caput; terris, pacibus, capitibus)
What three things must adjectives modify their nouns in? Does this mean that the endings of adjectives will match the endings of nouns? (gender, case, and number. No, the endings do not necessarily match, because the endings for adjectives are not always the same as for nouns.)
